next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 15

By nine the next morning the schoolyard smelled of old mud baked back into dust, goat dung, dried fish from somebody's breakfast basket, and the hot iron smell tin roofs made when the sun started climbing. Men had dragged the school benches out under the veranda because the two rooms inside could not hold the magistrate, the clerks, the disputed households, the watchers at the windows, and the bodies that had come simply because shame was worth seeing when it belonged to somebody else. Halima stood with the oilcloth ledger under her arm and felt the notebook's corners through the cloth, hard as the shoulder blades of starved children.

Mofiz had arrived early enough to arrange hospitality. A bamboo table stood under a white sheet. Three glasses of tea sweated rings onto a plastic tray. He had found an electric pedestal fan from somewhere on the eastern side and placed it so it turned first toward the magistrate's chair, then toward his own, then away from the crowd entirely. His beard edges were freshly dyed, too black against the gray in his cheeks, and every few minutes he patted the front of his panjabi as though the cloth itself might testify for him.

Aminul stood beside the table with his folders held off his chest so the papers would not drink his sweat. He had changed into a clean pale shirt and still looked tired enough for the cuffs to seem like punishment. Nura was a little behind him, papers squared in both hands, a pencil tucked over one ear. She had drawn her hair tight enough to pull at the corners of her eyes. Each time the stack slipped even h...
open_hooks_to_carry: Sufia's betrayal is motivated by fear and misjudgment, not simple malice.
The copied pages connect Halima's merciful forgeries to Mofiz's opportunistic fraud.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...ethod to offer. Her own life disproved cleanliness. She wanted the magistrate to hear the mess of dependency exactly as it was lived: who carried water, who slept by whose wall, who came back hungry and was not turned away. She also knew what happened when men in offices met mess. They called it fraud and kept their pens dry.

Nura came to the doorway with the copied statements stacked against her chest. "Three households asked for rewrites," she said. Her voice was brisk enough to sound almost detached, but Halima knew the signs of strain in the girl now. She overcontrolled when frightened. "Khodeja fupu's bedding has been sent to Khaleka's cousin's hut on the west side. Munni and Tuli are going to stay near the tea stall. Bashir's stepson has been told to sleep on the boat till after the hearing, because apparently a child on water doesn't count the same."

She handed the papers to Aminul and went back inside before either of them replied.

Halima stood with chalk grit on her palm.

Across the yard, Khaleka's grandson crossed with Khodeja fupu's rolled mat on one shoulder and her pill tin tied into the bedding so it knocked against the bamboo frame at each step. Near the gate, Munni had Tuli by the wrist, the child's slate wrapped in the same scarf bundle, both of them already turned toward the tea stalls. Down at the ghat, Bashir's stepson dragged a bedding roll twice his own width through the dust, shoved it into a fishing boat, and climbed in after it with an enamel plate under his arm. He sat in the bow with his knees pulled up and did not look back toward the houses.
state_deltas_required: Identify who weaponized the missing ledger pages and decide whether secrecy still offers any protection.
Halima learns Mofiz used copied pages to harden his own claims and decides to deploy the ledger publicly rather than keep defending it as a private moral reserve.
